articles.title: How a New Medicine Fights Alzheimer's Disease
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered how a medicine called lecanemab helps people with Alzheimer's disease. Alzheimer's is a brain illness that causes memory loss and confusion. In this disease, harmful clumps called amyloid plaques build up in the brain and damage brain cells. The medicine lecanemab is an antibody that targets these plaques. Researchers found that a special part of the antibody, called the Fc fragment, is very important. This part activates tiny brain cells called microglia, which are like the brain's cleanup crew. When activated, microglia grab onto the plaques and remove them, helping to slow down the disease. This discovery helps scientists understand how the medicine works and could lead to better treatments in the future. The study was done by scientists from VIB and KU Leuven and published in a science journal.

articles.title: A Giant Hole on a Metal Asteroid
articles.summary: Scientists are studying a big asteroid called Psyche in space between Mars and Jupiter. It is made mostly of metal and is very large. NASA is sending a spacecraft to visit it in 2029 to learn more. Researchers think Psyche might be the leftover heart of an old planet that broke apart, or it could have formed as a metal object from the start. To find out, they use computer simulations to look at a huge crater on Psyche's surface. This crater can show what is inside the asteroid, like if it has layers or is mixed up. Understanding Psyche helps us learn how planets like Earth were made long ago.

articles.title: UK Invests Money to Keep Tech Companies at Home
articles.summary: The UK government is spending £2.5 billion on new technologies like quantum computing and AI to stop British tech companies from moving to other countries. Chancellor Rachel Reeves says this investment will help the economy grow and create jobs. Many tech firms leave the UK because they can get more money and better deals abroad, especially in the US. The government wants to make the UK a great place for these companies to stay and succeed. This plan is part of a bigger effort to boost the economy and work more closely with other countries in Europe.

articles.title: ADHD Brains Take Tiny Naps While Awake
articles.summary: Scientists studied how brains sometimes act like they are sleeping even when people are awake. They looked at adults with ADHD, a condition that makes it hard to pay attention. The study found that people with ADHD have more of these sleep-like brain moments than others. These moments are linked to times when they lose focus, make more mistakes, or feel sleepy. The researchers think this might explain why ADHD causes attention problems. They also talked about using sounds during sleep to help reduce these brain naps and improve focus during the day. ADHD affects many people and involves trouble with attention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity.

articles.title: New Vaccines Made from Tiny DNA Shapes
articles.summary: Scientists have created a new type of vaccine using tiny shapes made from DNA, called DNA origami. This is different from mRNA vaccines, which became famous during the COVID-19 pandemic. DNA origami vaccines are designed to help the body fight viruses like COVID-19, HIV, and Ebola. They work by showing parts of the virus to the immune system, which then learns to attack it. In tests with mice and human cells, these vaccines made strong immune responses. They are also easier to store and make than mRNA vaccines, which need very cold temperatures. Researchers hope this new technology can help protect people from many diseases in the future.

articles.title: Scientists Say No to Removing Climate Info
articles.summary: A group of scientists called the National Academies of Sciences made a book to help judges understand science in court cases. This book has a new part about climate change, which explains how human activities are warming the Earth. Some politicians from certain states did not like this and asked the scientists to take out the climate change part. The scientists said no because they followed the same rules for all parts of the book. They want judges to learn about climate science to make fair decisions in court. Other scientists who wrote different parts of the book also supported keeping the climate change information, saying it is important for judges to know the facts without political interference. This shows that science should be shared openly, even when some people disagree.
